content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1 2 4 K Street
Sacramento 95814
(916) 322-5660

November 26, 1975

Bartley C. Deamer
McCutchen, Doyle, Brown & Enersen
601 California Street
San Francisco 94108

Re: Request for Opinion
No. 75-199

Dear Mr. Deamer:

Thank you for your letter of November 12, 1975,
regarding Regulation Section 18239(e)(2)(B). Your
questions have been considered by the Commission, and
I hope the following comments will be helpful.

(1) The requirements of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18239(e)(2)(B) are directed toward receipt of the required $1,000. If a person receives or becomes entitled
to receive $1,000 during a filing period as specified by
Section 18239(e)(2)(B), that individual is a "lobbyist
within the meaning of Government Code Section 82039. This
provision applies whether or not the individual has actually
engaged in direct communication. Consequently, a person
who receives the requisite $1,000 may be required to register
as a lobbyist even if the expected communication never occurs.

(2) 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18239(e)(2)(B) requires
that $1,000 be received "for the purpose of communicating
directly...". The direct communication and the activities
for which $1,000 was earned must relate to each other. Thus,
in the hypothetical situation you pose, the speech writer
who has a brief direct communication on an unrelated topic
need not register as a lobbyist.

(3) The $1,000 threshold excludes wages of full-
time employees engaged primarily to perform services other
than influencing or attempting to influence legislative or
administrative action. This exclusion applies to full-time
officers in other case of corporations and full-time partners
in the case of partnerships.

(4) In judging the primary purpose for which an
employee is engaged, only the activities which constitute

Bartley C. Deaner
November , 1975
Page Two

"influencing or attempting to influence legislative or
administrative action" under the Political Reform Act
need be considered. If an employee earns $1,000 on a
California matter during a particular period, he comes
within the provisions of Section 18239(c)(2)(B), even if
he is engaged primarily to influence legislative or admin-
istrative action in New York State. If he earns less than
$1,000 on a California matter during that period, he does
not come within the provisions of Section 18239(c)(2)(B).

If you have any questions, please contact Kenneth
Finney, Director of the Legal Division.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman, for the
Commission

DHL:jp

McCUTCHEN, DOYLE, BROWN & ENERSEN
COUNSELORS AT LAW
220 CALIFORNIA STREET
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94100

November 12, 1975

Mr. Rudy Nothenberg
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Nothenberg:

Thank you for taking the time this Monday to
discuss the operation of the $1,000 per 30-day period
threshold for lobbyist status. I understand, based on
our conversation, that the use of the phrase "for the pur-
pose of" in Regulation 18239(c)(2)(a) means that a wider
range of activities counts toward the $1,000 threshold than
the 10-hour threshold under Regulation 18239(c)(2)(c), al-
though not as wide a range of activities as the correspond-
ing provision of the emergency regulation which preceded it.
To cross the $1,000 threshold, however, the individual con-
cerned must in fact have some direct contact with legisla-
tive, administrative or state elected officials, and the
exclusion of travel and research time under Regulation
18239(c)(4) applies in allocating compensation by time
spent for purposes of computing the $1,000 threshold.

All the above is subject, of course, to the gen-
eral exclusion of wages earned by a full-time employee
engaged primarily to perform services other than influenc-
ing or attempting to influence legislative action.

After we spoke, four related questions occurred
to me which I thought you might also find appropriate
for answering on an informal basis.

1. When must the necessary communication by the
person earning $1,000 occur? Must it occur within the 30-
day period during which $1,000 was earned, or is it suffi-
cient that the $1,000 is earned in the expectation that
there would be direct communication at some future period?
If the latter, is it significant that the expected communi-
cation never occurs?

Mr. Rudy Nothenberg                    -2-                    November 12, 1975


     2.  Must the communication and the activity for which the $1,000 was earned relate to each other?  Am I correct, for example, in assuming that a speechwriter who earns $1,000 by preparing a presentation for another person need not register as a lobbyist if he should have a brief direct communication with an official on an unrelated topic?

     3.  Does the exclusion of full-time employees apply equally to full-time officers (in the case of corporations) and full-time partners (in the case of partnerships) who primarily act for the corporation or partnership, as the case may be, for purposes other than influencing or attempting to influence legislative or administrative action?

     4.  In judging the primary purpose for which an employee (or officer or partner) is engaged, need only the activities which constitute "influencing or attempting to influence legislative or administrative action" under the Political Reform Act of 1974 be considered?  For example, if an employee is engaged primarily to act as advocate before the legislature and administrative agencies of New York State, must he register under the Act if during a particular period he should earn $1,000 on a California matter?

     Any guidance on these questions which you can give would be appreciated.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        McCUTCHEN, DOYLE, BROWN & ENERSEN

                                        By [signature]
                                           Bartley C. Desmer
